file.content:
---
name: nexus-sites
description: Discover and show all local and WP Engine sites with status. Groups running vs halted, shows WPE links, and surfaces next actions. Use at the start of any workflow involving sites — always call this first before acting on a site.
allowed-tools: Bash(nexus *)
---

# Site Discovery

```!
nexus sites list
```

Present the results clearly:

1. **Running local sites** (ready to use) — name, domain, WPE link if any
2. **Halted local sites** — name, domain (note: must start before most operations)
3. **WP Engine installs** (cloud) — install name, environment (production/staging/dev), domain

If a site argument was provided (`$ARGUMENTS`), focus on that specific site and show its details with `nexus sites get $ARGUMENTS`.

After listing, identify what the user most likely wants to do next and suggest the relevant command.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: nexus-wpe
3	description: Query WP Engine account data — sites, installs, usage metrics, bandwidth, visits, storage, domains, backups, SSL, users, and diagnostics. Use when asked about WPE environments, usage stats, storage, installs, traffic, or anything related to the WP Engine account.
4	argument-hint: [topic] e.g. usage, installs, domains, backups, users, diagnose
5	allowed-tools: Bash(nexus *)
6	---
7	
8	# WP Engine Account Data
9	
10	Topic: `$ARGUMENTS`
11	
12	## Step 1: Get fleet-wide context
13	
14	```!
15	nexus wpe portfolio
16	```
17	
18	This shows all accounts, total installs, and fleet-wide visits + bandwidth.
19	Use this data to answer usage/traffic/storage questions directly when possible.
20	Only run additional commands if more detail is needed.
21	
22	## Step 2: Fetch additional detail if needed
23	
24	Based on `$ARGUMENTS` and what the portfolio output didn't cover:
25	
26	| Topic | Command |
27	|-------|---------|
28	| **Usage / bandwidth / visits (fleet)** | ✅ Already in portfolio above |
29	| Usage drill-down (per account) | `nexus wpe account-usage <accountId>` |
30	| Usage drill-down (per install) | `nexus wpe usage <installId>` |
31	| Prior month usage | `nexus wpe portfolio --month-offset 1` |
32	| Account overview | `nexus wpe account <accountId>` |
33	| Account limits | `nexus wpe limits <accountId>` |
34	| All installs for an account | `nexus wpe installs <accountId>` |
35	| Install details | `nexus wpe install <installId>` |
36	| Domains | `nexus wpe domains <installId>` |
37	| SSL status | `nexus wpe ssl <installId>` |
38	| Backups | `nexus wpe backup-status <installId> <backupId>` |
39	| Users | `nexus wpe users <accountId>` |
40	| Diagnose install | `nexus wpe diagnose <installId>` |
41	| Go-live check | `nexus wpe go-live-check <installId> <domain>` |
42	
43	**If the portfolio shows zeros** for visits/bandwidth, the current billing period
44	hasn't rolled up yet. Run: `nexus wpe portfolio --month-offset 1` to get last month's data.
45	
46	## Step 3: Present findings
47	
48	- Highlight accounts with the most traffic or storage
49	- Flag anything abnormal (high bandwidth, 404 accounts, missing SSL)
50	- Suggest follow-up if user wants per-install breakdown
51

file.content:
---
name: nexus-wpe
description: Query WP Engine account data — sites, installs, usage metrics, bandwidth, visits, storage, domains, backups, SSL, users, and diagnostics. Use when asked about WPE environments, usage stats, storage, installs, traffic, or anything related to the WP Engine account.
argument-hint: [topic] e.g. usage, installs, domains, backups, users, diagnose
allowed-tools: Bash(nexus *)
---

# WP Engine Account Data

Topic: `$ARGUMENTS`

## Step 1: Get fleet-wide context

```!
nexus wpe portfolio
```

This shows all accounts, total installs, and fleet-wide visits + bandwidth.
Use this data to answer usage/traffic/storage questions directly when possible.
Only run additional commands if more detail is needed.

## Step 2: Fetch additional detail if needed

Based on `$ARGUMENTS` and what the portfolio output didn't cover:

| Topic | Command |
|-------|---------|
| **Usage / bandwidth / visits (fleet)** | ✅ Already in portfolio above |
| Usage drill-down (per account) | `nexus wpe account-usage <accountId>` |
| Usage drill-down (per install) | `nexus wpe usage <installId>` |
| Prior month usage | `nexus wpe portfolio --month-offset 1` |
| Account overview | `nexus wpe account <accountId>` |
| Account limits | `nexus wpe limits <accountId>` |
| All installs for an account | `nexus wpe installs <accountId>` |
| Install details | `nexus wpe install <installId>` |
| Domains | `nexus wpe domains <installId>` |
| SSL status | `nexus wpe ssl <installId>` |
| Backups | `nexus wpe backup-status <installId> <backupId>` |
| Users | `nexus wpe users <accountId>` |
| Diagnose install | `nexus wpe diagnose <installId>` |
| Go-live check | `nexus wpe go-live-check <installId> <domain>` |

**If the portfolio shows zeros** for visits/bandwidth, the current billing period
hasn't rolled up yet. Run: `nexus wpe portfolio --month-offset 1` to get last month's data.

## Step 3: Present findings

- Highlight accounts with the most traffic or storage
- Flag anything abnormal (high bandwidth, 404 accounts, missing SSL)
- Suggest follow-up if user wants per-install breakdown

1	---
2	name: nexus-wp
3	description: Run WP-CLI commands on a local WordPress site via nexus. Target format is <sitename>@local. For WPE remote installs use nexus-wpe skill instead.
4	argument-hint: <site@local> <wp-cli-command>
5	allowed-tools: Bash(nexus *)
6	---
7	
8	# WP-CLI via Nexus (local sites)
9	
10	For **local** sites only. WPE remote installs → use `nexus-wpe` skill.
11	
12	Target format: `<sitename>@local` or bare site name (auto-resolved from graph DB).
13	
14	**Common commands:**
15	
16	```
17	nexus wp core version <target>          # WP version
18	nexus wp plugin list <target>           # list plugins
19	nexus wp plugin update <target> --all   # update all plugins
20	nexus wp health <target>                # site health check
21	nexus wp option-get <target> siteurl    # get WP option
22	nexus wp user-list <target>             # list users
23	nexus wp db export <target>             # export database
24	```
25	
26	**Examples:**
27	```
28	nexus wp core version mysite@local
29	nexus wp core version jppblank          # bare name — auto-resolves local or WPE
30	nexus wp plugin list mysite@local --json
31	```
32	
33	Show the output and explain what it means.
34

file.content:
---
name: nexus-wp
description: Run WP-CLI commands on a local WordPress site via nexus. Target format is <sitename>@local. For WPE remote installs use nexus-wpe skill instead.
argument-hint: <site@local> <wp-cli-command>
allowed-tools: Bash(nexus *)
---

# WP-CLI via Nexus (local sites)

For **local** sites only. WPE remote installs → use `nexus-wpe` skill.

Target format: `<sitename>@local` or bare site name (auto-resolved from graph DB).

**Common commands:**

```
nexus wp core version <target>          # WP version
nexus wp plugin list <target>           # list plugins
nexus wp plugin update <target> --all   # update all plugins
nexus wp health <target>                # site health check
nexus wp option-get <target> siteurl    # get WP option
nexus wp user-list <target>             # list users
nexus wp db export <target>             # export database
```

**Examples:**
```
nexus wp core version mysite@local
nexus wp core version jppblank          # bare name — auto-resolves local or WPE
nexus wp plugin list mysite@local --json
```

Show the output and explain what it means.

content.text:
Here's what I found:

**Yes, you likely do have a local copy.** The site `local-copy-jpp` is running and linked to a WPE production install — the name strongly suggests it's a copy of your `jpp`-prefixed WPE site.

**Local copy (`local-copy-jpp`) status:**
- Status: Running at `http://localhost:10033`
- WordPress: 6.9.4
- 117 published posts, last post dated **5/2/2025**
- 20 active / 28 installed plugins
- Last indexed: 4/30/2026 (today)
- Linked to WPE install: `7576b9e6-64de-45fd-809f-9fd659a91cc1` (production)

**How in sync are they?**

I can't answer that precisely right now — WPE authentication is failing, so I can't pull live data from the `jppblank` install to compare post counts, plugin versions, or WordPress version.

To fix that, run `nexus wpe login` or authenticate via Local's UI, then I can compare the two directly and tell you exactly what's drifted.

One thing I can tell from the local data: if the live site has had any posts or plugin updates since **May 2, 2025**, the local copy is behind by roughly a year.

result:
Here's what I found:

**Yes, you likely do have a local copy.** The site `local-copy-jpp` is running and linked to a WPE production install — the name strongly suggests it's a copy of your `jpp`-prefixed WPE site.

**Local copy (`local-copy-jpp`) status:**
- Status: Running at `http://localhost:10033`
- WordPress: 6.9.4
- 117 published posts, last post dated **5/2/2025**
- 20 active / 28 installed plugins
- Last indexed: 4/30/2026 (today)
- Linked to WPE install: `7576b9e6-64de-45fd-809f-9fd659a91cc1` (production)

**How in sync are they?**

I can't answer that precisely right now — WPE authentication is failing, so I can't pull live data from the `jppblank` install to compare post counts, plugin versions, or WordPress version.

To fix that, run `nexus wpe login` or authenticate via Local's UI, then I can compare the two directly and tell you exactly what's drifted.

One thing I can tell from the local data: if the live site has had any posts or plugin updates since **May 2, 2025**, the local copy is behind by roughly a year.
